summary: Jeff Atwood satirically proposes 'Bad Code Offsets' as a tongue-in-cheek alternative to the hard work of actually becoming a better programmer. The concept parodies carbon offsets, allowing developers to assuage their guilt about producing bad code by donating money to open source projects. While presented humorously, the initiative has a real purpose: all proceeds go to organizations like jQuery, PostgreSQL, and the Apache Software Foundation. Atwood acknowledges that improving as a programmer through apprenticeship, practice, and community involvement is genuinely difficult work. The satire serves as both a fundraising mechanism for open source and a gentle critique of developers who don't invest in self-improvement.
key_points: Becoming a better programmer requires genuinely hard work including apprenticeship, practice, and community involvement
The 'Bad Code Offsets' concept cleverly parodies carbon offset programs while serving a real charitable purpose
All money from bad code offsets goes directly to open source projects like jQuery, PostgreSQL, and Apache
The goal is to subsidize quality open source code to counterbalance the bad code programmers inevitably produce
The satire gently mocks the desire for easy solutions to problems that require sustained effort
money_quotes: Let's face it: we all write bad code.
But that's a lot of work. Really freaking hard work!
It is, quite literally, the least you could do.
Bad Code Offsets provides the vehicle for balancing the scales of poor past practice while freeing us to pursue current excellence in code development.
For every ten thousand lines of mind-bendingly bad code produced, we hope to subsidize a thousand lines of quality open source code.
key_insight: Self-improvement as a programmer is genuinely hard work, but supporting open source projects is a worthy way to contribute to code quality even if you can't eliminate your own bad code.
